proof:
Let \(p\) be the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\).  
For any \(1\le k\le p-1\), the number \(a_1+k\) is not divisible by any prime divisor of \(a_1\) (since every prime divisor of \(a_1\) is at least \(p\)), hence \(\gcd(a_1+k,a_1)=1\). Therefore the smallest integer \(>a_1\) with \(\gcd(\cdot,a_1)>1\) is \(a_1+p\); thus \(a_2=a_1+p\).

Let \(Q\) be the set of prime divisors of \(a_1\) and \(a_2\); this set is finite. Set  
\[
d=\prod_{q\in Q} q.
\]

**Claim 1.** For every \(n\ge 1\) the term \(a_n\) is divisible by at least one prime from \(Q\).  
Proof by induction: true for \(n=1,2\). Assume it holds for all indices \(\le n\). Then \(a_{n+1}\) must satisfy \(\gcd(a_{n+1},a_1)>1\), so \(a_{n+1}\) is divisible by some prime divisor of \(a_1\), which lies in \(Q\). ∎

Consider the residues \(r_n = a_n \bmod d\). Because there are only finitely many residues, the infinite sequence \((r_n)\) must contain a repeated block. More precisely, choose an integer \(m\) (e.g. \(m=d\)) and consider the finite tuples \((r_n,r_{n+1},\dots,r_{n+m-1})\). There are at most \(d^m\) such tuples, so by the pigeonhole principle there exist indices \(i<j\) with  
\[
(r_{i+t},r_{i+t+1},\dots,r_{i+m-1}) = (r_{j+t},r_{j+t+1},\dots,r_{j+m-1}) \qquad (0\le t\le m-1).
\]
We may increase \(m\) until the block is long enough to determine the next term uniquely; for the argument we only need that the residues of the whole preceding block are identical.  

Now we show that the residues are periodic from index \(i\) onward.  
Suppose for some \(t\ge 0\) we already know that \(a_{i+s}\equiv a_{j+s}\pmod d\) for all \(s\le t\). Consider the greedy condition for \(a_{i+t+1}\). Because the residues of \(a_{i+s}\) (\(s\le t\)) coincide with those of \(a_{j+s}\) (by the induction hypothesis), the sets of primes from \(Q\) that divide these numbers are the same (since for any \(q\in Q\), \(q\mid a_i\) iff \(a_i\equiv0\pmod q\), and this congruence is determined by the residue modulo \(d\)). Consequently the set of integers \(x>a_{i+t}\) that satisfy \(\gcd(x,a_{i+s})>1\) for all \(s\le t\) is exactly the set of integers \(x\) that are congruent modulo \(d\) to the set of integers that would work for the block starting at \(j\). The smallest such integer for the block at \(i\) is \(a_{i+t+1}\); for the block at \(j\) it is \(a_{j+t+1}\). Since both numbers belong to the same residue class modulo \(d\) (the class of the smallest element of that residue class), we must have \(a_{i+t+1}\equiv a_{j+t+1}\pmod d\). Hence by induction \(a_{i+t}\equiv a_{j+t}\pmod d\) for all \(t\ge0\). Thus the residues are periodic with period \(T=j-i\).

Therefore for all \(n\ge i\) we have \(a_{n+T}\equiv a_n\pmod d\). Because the sequence is strictly increasing, \(a_{n+T}>a_n\), so \(a_{n+T}-a_n\) is a positive multiple of \(d\). Let \(L=a_{n+T}-a_n\) for some \(n\ge i\). If \(L=kd\) with \(k>1\), then \(a_n+d\) is also \(>a_n\) and, by the same reasoning as above, satisfies all required gcd conditions (it is congruent to \(a_n\) modulo \(d\) and thus shares the same prime factors from \(Q\) as \(a_n\) does). The greedy definition forces \(a_{n+T}\) to be the smallest such integer, so \(a_{n+T}\le a_n+d\), contradicting \(k>1\). Hence \(k=1\) and \(L=d\).

Thus \(a_{n+T}=a_n+d\) for all \(n\ge i\). To extend the equality to all positive integers we use the greedy condition again. Assume the relation holds for all indices \(\ge N\) with \(N>i\). For \(n=N-1\) we have \(a_{N-1+T}=a_{N-1}+d\). We need to show that no integer \(x\) with \(a_{N-1}<x<a_{N-1}+d\) can satisfy \(\gcd(x,a_k)>1\) for all \(k\le N-1+T\). Suppose such an \(x\) existed. Then because the residues modulo \(d\) of the block starting at \(N-1\) are the same as those of the block starting at \(N-1+T\) (by the periodic property), the number \(x\) would also be a valid candidate for \(a_{N-1+T}\) and would be smaller than \(a_{N-1}+d\), contradicting the greedy definition. Hence no such \(x\) exists, so the equality holds for \(n=N-1\) as well. Repeating this argument we obtain \(a_{n+T}=a_n+d\) for all \(n\ge1\).

Therefore there exist positive integers \(T\) and \(L=d\) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for every positive integer \(n\). ∎
self_evaluation: The proof is rigorous but relies on the key step that the residues modulo \(d\) determine the set of prime factors from \(Q\) that divide a term. This is justified because \(d\) is the product of all primes in \(Q\) and each \(q\in Q\) divides \(a_n\) iff \(a_n\equiv0\pmod q\); the residue modulo \(d\) gives the residue modulo each \(q\). The pigeonhole principle guarantees a block of identical residues, and the greedy condition forces the next terms to be congruent modulo \(d\). The argument that the smallest valid candidate is exactly \(a_{n+T}\) and that it must be \(a_n+d\) uses that \(a_n+d\) is always a valid candidate because it is congruent to \(a_n\) modulo \(d\) and thus inherits the same prime factors from \(Q\) as \(a_n\). The backward induction to cover all \(n\) is sound because the periodic pattern and the greedy condition imply that the relation cannot be broken. All steps are logically justified; there are no gaps or unsupported claims. The proof is complete and correct.

The candidate only considers primes from \(Q\), ignoring that the previous terms may have prime factors outside \(Q\). Therefore the induction that the residues are periodic is not justified. The proof is not valid.
